titleStmt.title: The Director of the Office of International Trade Policy ( Wilcox ) to the Assistant Secretary of State for Economic Affairs ( Clayton )Marginal notation by John M. Leddy, Adviser on general commercial policy, Division of Commercial Policy: “Read to Mr. Clayton by Mr. Wilcox, Apr. 3, ’46”.

div.head: The Director of the Office of International Trade Policy ( Wilcox ) to the Assistant Secretary of State for Economic Affairs ( Clayton )Marginal notation by John M. Leddy, Adviser on general commercial policy, Division of Commercial Policy: “Read to Mr. Clayton by Mr. Wilcox, Apr. 3, ’46”.
div.p: We have reached the point in our trade program where a definite date (subject to change only if the British loan fails or is unduly delayed)For documentation on this subject, see Foreign Relations, 1945, vol. vi, pp. 1 ff. must be set for the preliminary trade meeting. Physical and other preparations for the meeting require this, and the British have asked us for an immediate answer.

When a date is agreed upon, the British plan to announce it in Parliament together with an invitation to industry and trade (in general terms without mention of specific products) to express their views on concessions to be asked and offered.

I recommend that we set September 1 as the definite date for the opening of the meeting. This would mean that our public notice of intention to negotiate should be issued not later than June 1. A detailed time-table is set out in the attached memorandum.

Before we commit ourselves to this time-table, however, I think we should take precautions to be sure we can carry through notwithstanding the pressures which are certain to develop when our public notice is issued.

You have read Harry Hawkins’ letter to you on this subject. Two
points stand out. 1) We must be sure that, once we have agreed to September 1, we are not going to let the fear of political pressures result in postponement of the public notice until after the elections. 2) We must be sure that such pressures in the months following public notice will not be permitted to hamper the operation of the trade-agreements organization and thus defeat our objectives.

With this in mind, I have prepared the attached memorandum to the PresidentDraft not printed; see memorandum of April 4, p. 1307. which I feel should be discussed personally with him by the Secretary or the Acting Secretary and yourself. Only in this way, I believe, can we be reasonably certain that we will not be saddled with commitments that could wreck the whole program.

p.hi: Time-table of Preparations for Preliminary Meeting on Trade and Employment

frus:attachment.p: The following is a tentative time-table for completion of the various stages of preparation for the preliminary meeting on trade and employment.

row.cell: Transmittal of draft Charter of ITO to TAC and subcommittees of ECEFP. This has been done.
TAC and subcommittees of ECEFP to complete consideration of draft Charter.
Transmittal of revised draft Charter to ECEFP.
Issuance of public notice of intention to negotiate.
Transmittal of Charter recommended by ECEFP to countries members of ECOSOC Preparatory Committee (prior clearance with President).
Closing date for submission of briefs and applications to be heard.
Preparatory Committee to begin consideration of non-trade barrier provisions of Charter.
Final recommendations to President as a result of public hearings.
